common_name: Autumn Cattleya (Crimson Cattleya, Ruby Orchid, Purple Queen Orchid)

planting.note: Grow in slatted orchid pots or baskets with coarse bark media in bright indirect to direct morning sun; Cattleya labiata needs bright light (bright indirect to filtered sun) to bloom reliably and will not flower in dim conditions. Cannot tolerate frost — minimum 50°F. In zone 9, grow year-round on a bright, sheltered porch or indoors at a south or east window. Natural blooming occurs in fall (September–November in the Northern Hemisphere). A summer rest with reduced watering (not complete drought) encourages flower spike development. Repot every 2–3 years. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to filtered direct light — Cattleya labiata requires more light than most houseplants but less than most outdoor ornamentals; bright filtered sun or a south-facing window with light curtain is ideal. Cattleya labiata is a spectacular epiphytic orchid endemic to a restricted range in northeastern Brazil (Pernambuco, Alagoas, and adjacent states), discovered by William Swainson and introduced to cultivation in England in 1818. The first flowers opened at William Cattley's greenhouse in Barnet in 1824, causing a sensation — the large (5–8 inch), wavy, deep lavender-purple flowers with a ruffled, velvety, deep magenta-crimson lip were unlike anything previously seen in European horticulture. The plant was named Cattleya labiata in Cattley's honor, and the entire genus was named for him. Cattleya labiata became the parent of thousands of hybrid orchids over the following two centuries — the traditional 'corsage orchid' of the 20th century is a Cattleya hybrid, and C. labiata's influence runs through the vast majority of the large-flowered Cattleya orchid hybrids still popular today. The fall-blooming season (September–November in the Northern Hemisphere) gives the plant its common name 'Autumn Cattleya.' The flowers are waxy, intensely fragrant, and extraordinarily beautiful. Non-toxic to pets.
sources: American Orchid Society — Cattleya labiata cultivation and history
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Cattleya labiata: species and history
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cattleya orchids for indoor culture
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Cattleya orchids (non-toxic)

common_name: Strict Tillandsia (Rosy Air Plant, Brazilian Air Plant, Stricta)

planting.note: Mount on cork bark, wood, or wire in bright indirect to gentle direct light; Tillandsia stricta is one of the more cold-tolerant air plants, surviving temperatures to 28–30°F briefly when dry. In zone 9, plants can be grown outdoors year-round in sheltered positions with good air circulation. Water by dunking in water for 20–30 minutes or misting thoroughly every 2–3 days; allow to dry completely within 4 hours. Produces abundant pups after blooming, quickly forming dense, attractive clumps. Excellent for mounting in groups on boards or logs. Non-toxic.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to gentle direct morning sun — Tillandsia stricta adapts to a range of light conditions better than most Tillandsias and is one of the recommended starter air plants for outdoor use in zone 8-9. Tillandsia stricta is a compact, clump-forming epiphytic bromeliad native to Venezuela, Trinidad, Guyana, Suriname, French Guiana, Brazil, and Argentina — a very wide natural range spanning multiple climate zones that explains its reputation as one of the most adaptable and cold-tolerant of the commonly grown Tillandsias. The plant forms a small (4–6 inch) rosette of narrow, somewhat stiff, silvery-green leaves arranged in a spherical to fan-shaped pattern. At bloom time, the rosette produces an upright flower head with bright pink to red bracts from which small, deep blue-violet tubular flowers emerge — a brilliant pink-and-purple color combination that makes the blooming plant particularly striking. After blooming, T. stricta is among the most prolific producers of offsets — a well-established clump can develop 5–10 pups from a single parent, forming a large, multi-rosette cluster in a relatively short time. In zone 8-9, mounted clumps of T. stricta grown outdoors year-round on tree branches, drift wood, or bark panels have become a popular low-maintenance ornamental feature in sheltered garden areas.
sources: Bromeliad Society International — Tillandsia stricta cultivation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tillandsia species for Gulf Coast
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Cold-tolerant air plants for zone 8-9
American Orchid Society — Tillandsia cultivation guide

common_name: Queen's Tears Bromeliad (Friendship Plant, Nodding Air Plant)

planting.note: Plant in partial shade to full sun in well-draining, slightly acidic potting mix or garden soil; Queen's Tears Bromeliad is the most cold-hardy ornamental bromeliad widely available, surviving temperatures to 20–25°F with heavy mulch protection — reliable in zone 8 and very easy in zone 9. In zone 9, grow outdoors as a permanent ground cover, container specimen, or shade garden accent; it naturalizes easily under trees. Keep the central cup filled with water. Divide crowded clumps every 2–3 years by separating rooted offsets. One of the best-sharing plants — divisions and pups establish quickly; hence the common name 'Friendship Plant' reflecting the tradition of sharing it among friends.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in well-draining, moderately fertile soil; Queen's Tears Bromeliad is highly adaptable to a range of light conditions and is one of the most forgiving and easy-to-grow of all bromeliads, making it an excellent choice for gardeners new to the bromeliad family. Billbergia nutans is a clump-forming, tank bromeliad native to Brazil, Uruguay, Paraguay, and Argentina — one of the more southerly-ranging bromeliads, explaining its exceptional cold tolerance relative to other family members. The plant forms erect, tube-like rosettes of dark-green, finely toothed, narrow leaves that grow in dense, spreading colonies. In late winter to spring (and sometimes in fall), the rosettes produce a nodding (the 'nutans' — 'nodding') flower stalk that arches downward, carrying pendant clusters of remarkable, multicolored flowers: drooping green petals edged in blue, emerging from vivid pink bracts, with prominent yellow stamens — a combination of pink, blue, green, and yellow in a single flower cluster that is unique among commonly grown plants. The flowers last 1–2 weeks but are produced abundantly, and established colonies may flower multiple times per year. Among the hardiest, most adaptable, and most readily available bromeliads for zone 8-9 outdoor gardens.
sources: Bromeliad Society International — Billbergia nutans cultivation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Billbergia for Florida landscapes
LSU AgCenter — Cold-Hardy Bromeliads for Gulf Coast Louisiana
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Billbergia nutans for zone 8-9 outdoor gardens

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in late spring in full sun with well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Calabash Tree is frost-sensitive and best suited to zone 10-11 for outdoor planting. In zone 9, container culture is practical for young trees. The tree blooms on old wood (trunk and major branches), so pruning removes future fruiting wood — prune minimally. The large, round fruits (4–14 inches in diameter) hang directly from the trunk and branches by short stems in a phenomenon called 'cauliflory.' Fruits are ornamentally striking but the pulp is mildly toxic and used medicinally, not eaten fresh.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile soil — Calabash Tree grows most vigorously with full sun and regular water during establishment, then tolerates periods of drought in sandy and rocky soils once established. Crescentia cujete is a medium-sized tropical tree native to Central America, the Caribbean, and northern South America, widely distributed by pre-Columbian peoples throughout tropical America as a utilitarian and sacred plant — the large, round, hard-shelled fruits of the calabash have been among the most widely used natural containers, bowls, cups, ladles, and musical instrument bodies across all cultures of the tropical Americas for thousands of years. The tree is immediately recognizable by its phenomenon of 'cauliflory' — flowers and fruits borne directly on the trunk and major branches rather than on the growing tips, a strategy that attracts bat pollinators that can access the flowers and fruits by hovering next to the trunk. The greenish-white, bell-shaped flowers open at night for bat pollination, and the mature fruits are perfectly round to slightly ovoid, 4–14 inches in diameter, hanging directly from the bark on short stalks. The fruit shell is extraordinarily hard when dried — traditionally cut in half for bowls, carved and decorated as art objects, or used as maracas. In zone 10-11 Gulf Coast and South Florida landscapes, the Calabash Tree is a distinctive tropical ornamental valued for its unusual fruiting display.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Crescentia cujete (Calabash Tree)
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Crescentia cujete cultivation notes
Missouri Botanical Garden — Crescentia cujete species profile
Smithsonian Tropical Research Institute — Cauliflory and bat pollination in Crescentia

common_name: Chinese Trumpet Vine (Chinese Trumpet Creeper, Grandiflora Trumpet Vine)

planting.note: Plant container-grown vines in spring on a very sturdy trellis, fence, pergola, or arbor in full sun to partial shade; Campsis grandiflora grows vigorously and produces vines too heavy for lightweight supports. Unlike C. radicans, C. grandiflora generally does not self-adhere to surfaces and requires tying to its support. It blooms on new wood — prune in late winter before growth begins for maximum bloom. In zone 9, it blooms from summer through fall. A significant hummingbird plant. Less aggressively spreading by root suckers than C. radicans.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Chinese Trumpet Vine blooms most prolifically in full sun but tolerates and even benefits from afternoon shade in zone 9's intense summer heat, extending the blooming season. Campsis grandiflora is a vigorous, deciduous, woody climbing vine native to China and Japan in the Bignoniaceae family, closely related to the American Trumpet Vine (Campsis radicans) but producing flowers roughly twice the size — 3–4 inches across rather than the 2-inch flowers of its American relative. The large, trumpet-shaped flowers are borne in terminal clusters of 6–12, in vivid shades of orange-red with a salmon-orange interior tube — somewhat less intensely red than C. radicans and more orange-apricot in tone. The flowers bloom from mid-summer through fall in successive flushes and are extraordinary hummingbird attractants. Unlike C. radicans, which clings to surfaces via aerial holdfast roots, C. grandiflora generally lacks strong self-clinging ability and requires tying to support structures — a quality that makes it more controllable in small garden spaces. The intergeneric hybrid Campsis × tagliabuana 'Madame Galen' (C. radicans × C. grandiflora) combines the best features of both parents and is one of the most widely planted trumpet vines in US gardens. Zone 7–9 hardiness makes C. grandiflora a better Gulf Coast choice than the more cold-hardy but aggressively spreading C. radicans.
sources: LSU AgCenter — Campsis grandiflora for Gulf Coast Louisiana
University of Georgia Extension — Campsis grandiflora for Southeast gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Campsis species for Gulf Coast zone 7-9
Royal Horticultural Society — Campsis grandiflora (AGM plant)
